websites.name: Linear
websites.design_notes: Split hero with real product UI on the right. Dark theme with #0D0D0F bg, near-black surfaces, and a single accent (purple #5E6AD2). Custom typography (Inter + Söhne). Motion is restrained — only the cursor in the product UI moves. Logo strip replaced with a single rotating customer quote. This is the reference for 'split hero with live product demo'.

websites.name: Vercel
websites.design_notes: Pure black background (#000) with high-contrast white text and a single saturated pink/red accent for primary CTAs. Hero uses a live deploy log or animated terminal-style element rather than a static screenshot. Typography is Geist Sans + Geist Mono, both designed in-house for maximum terminal-screen legibility. Lots of negative space and one large product image per scroll section.

websites.name: Stripe
websites.design_notes: Stripe's signature animated gradient hero background (the moving colorful blob mesh) defined an era of SaaS hero design. Layered on top: tight, full-bleed product screenshots in glassmorphic floating panels. Custom type (Söhne) with a wide range of weights. Heavy use of subtle background gradients on each section to create depth without flat color. Reference for 'how to make gradients feel premium not Web 2.0'.

websites.name: Framer
websites.design_notes: Hero features an interactive, drag-to-build canvas demo that lets visitors manipulate a real mini-Framer scene. Bright, slightly pastel color palette with blue primary. Heavy use of micro-interactions on every CTA (button hover reveals, magnetic hover). Custom illustrations with the same design language as the product. The marketing site itself is built in Framer, which is the proof of concept.

websites.name: Cal.com
websites.design_notes: Clean light/dark mode default with a confident blue accent (#2962FF) and a red secondary (#E5484D). Hero features a real calendar booking UI mockup with motion showing date/time selection. Strong open-source messaging with GitHub stars visible in nav. Documentation heavily linked from homepage — Docs link is treated as a primary nav item, not buried.

websites.name: Supabase
websites.design_notes: Dark theme with Supabase green (#3ECF8E) as the primary accent — instantly recognizable. Hero uses a code snippet block on the right showing how easy Supabase is to use, with syntax highlighting matching the brand green. Heavy use of monospace for code and developer credibility details (PR counts, contributors). The whole site feels like a PostgreSQL dev wrote it.

websites.name: Railway
websites.design_notes: Purple-dominant dark theme with a distinctive product visualization — a node-graph UI showing services connected together. Hero animation shows deploy pipeline in real time. Monospace headers (IBM Plex Mono) give it a railway-system engineering vibe. Layout breaks the typical 3-column feature grid in favor of an asymmetric service graph.

websites.name: Bun
websites.design_notes: Bun takes the rare approach of a SaaS page that reads like a README. Big bench-chart at the top showing Bun's speed advantage over Node and Deno. Red Bun logo accent on a near-black bg with cream/white text. Heavy on benchmark numbers and code examples. Strong use of a single sans (Inter) and monospace (JetBrains Mono) with no decorative type.

websites.name: Notion
websites.design_notes: Friendly mascot-led design with the Notion emoji-style iconography. Pure white background with light gray surfaces (#F7F7F5) — no dark hero. Heavy use of real product screenshots showing documents being constructed. The page itself is built like a Notion doc with section headings, emoji icons, and callouts — meta design.

websites.name: Webflow
websites.design_notes: Hero uses a giant canvas-style background with abstract design tool UI elements layered over a saturated blue (#4353FF). Headlines are huge (72-96px), confident, in Webflow's custom display face. Page is section-dense with each section anchored to a different value prop — design freedom, CMS, ecommerce, hosting. Product UI screenshots are framed in realistic browser chrome.

websites.name: Plasmic
websites.design_notes: Plasmic uses a builder-canvas hero with floating UI panels and arrows showing the design-to-code flow. Colorful accents (yellow, pink, blue) on a near-white background. Headlines mix display serif with sans for a designed-in-Plasmic feel. Strong use of animated arrows and connectors between UI elements to communicate the workflow visually.

websites.name: Mintlify
websites.design_notes: Mint brand color (mint green) on white background with a docs-product emphasis. Hero features a real Mintlify-rendered docs page mockup on the right, showing the polished docs UI that's the product. Custom illustrations in a consistent line-style. Page is shorter than typical SaaS pages — confident brevity because the product speaks for itself in the screenshot.

websites.name: Resend
websites.design_notes: Near-black background with a single warm-white text color and one subtle gradient accent (orange-to-pink) used only on the logo and primary CTA. Hero is split: left has headline + CTA, right has a real email rendered in a code-style block. Heavy use of large pull-quotes from developers as social proof, no logo strip.

websites.name: Raycast
websites.design_notes: Raycast red (#FF6363) accent on a true-black background with the rest of the palette being neutrals. Hero features the Raycast command-bar UI floating center-screen with cursor moving through it — a literal demo of the product. Heavy use of the macOS window chrome aesthetic (rounded corners, traffic-light dots) on every screenshot. Store section shows community extensions like an app store.

websites.name: Tldraw
websites.design_notes: Tldraw is famous for putting a working whiteboard AS the hero — visitors can draw on it immediately. Light theme with hand-drawn-style accents (slight rotation, sketchy borders) on UI elements. Black-on-white with light blue (#1971C2) and pink (#E64980) as accent colors for the drawing tools. Open-source badge prominently displayed.

websites.name: PostHog
websites.design_notes: PostHog Hedgehog mascot is the brand — appears throughout the site in different illustrations. Purple-dominant palette (#1D4AFF primary) with a yellow secondary used on the mascot. Site uses an unusual purple-on-cream background section pattern in places. Heavy use of blog posts and tutorials surfaced on the homepage because the docs and content are the conversion funnel.

websites.name: Cursor
websites.design_notes: Dark theme with a single accent color (Cursor uses a soft purple/lavender). Hero features a real Cursor editor screenshot with AI prompt UI visible — proof-of-product. Layout is unusually sparse: huge headline, two buttons, one screenshot, then a long list of features in compact text. Avoids the typical 3-card feature grid entirely.

websites.name: Liveblocks
websites.design_notes: Dark theme with neon-multi-cursor visuals throughout (multiple colored cursors moving through UI mockups to convey collaboration). The brand color isn't one color — it's the rainbow of cursor colors. Each feature section is anchored by an interactive demo. Layout uses alternating left/right image-text splits.

websites.name: Pitch
websites.design_notes: Light theme with a confident black-on-white palette and a single bright accent color (Pitch uses a saturated purple). Hero shows a real Pitch presentation being edited collaboratively. Heavy use of slide-deck-style framing for feature sections — each feature is presented like a slide. Typography mixes a friendly display sans with a clean body sans.

websites.name: Fathom Analytics
websites.design_notes: Warm cream background (#FAF5EF) instead of pure white — a deliberate signal of being non-corporate and privacy-first. Single deep-blue accent (#0F4C81) used sparingly. Hero features a real Fathom dashboard screenshot. Copy is unusually long-form and conversational — the entire value prop is explained in 3-4 paragraphs above the fold.

websites.name: Lusion
websites.design_notes: Lusion is a WebGL-heavy creative studio site with full-screen 3D scenes that respond to scroll and mouse. Color palette is dominated by saturated pinks, blues, and yellows against dark backgrounds. Loading screen is a full experience itself — preloader with a custom 3D animation. Page transitions use Three.js camera moves between sections, not CSS fades.

websites.name: Bruno Simon
websites.design_notes: Famous portfolio where you drive a toy car around a 3D world to navigate — every project is a physical object you crash into. Bright primary colors (red, blue, yellow) on a green/grass-colored ground. Three.js + Cannon.js physics make every interaction feel tactile. Text is rendered as 3D extruded geometry, not HTML.

websites.name: Resende
websites.design_notes: Resende is a designer portfolio that uses huge typography (custom display sans, often 200px+ headers) as the primary visual element. Pure black-on-white with one accent color used only on hover. Navigation is a single horizontal menu — no hamburger, no mega-menu. Project pages are full-bleed image grids with tight 8px gutters.

websites.name: Jessica Hische
websites.design_notes: Hische's portfolio leads with her lettering work in a colorful card grid. Background is warm cream with each project card in a different saturated color. Strong use of her own hand-lettering as section headers — type IS the brand. Project pages mix process shots with final work, showing sketches and reference material alongside final pieces.

websites.name: Mike Kus
websites.design_notes: Kus uses earth-toned photography as the dominant visual — burnt oranges, warm grays, soft browns. Type is a single humanist sans (LFT Etica) used at multiple sizes with no display face. Hero is a large rotating image carousel, not a static shot. Project pages use editorial-style layouts with mixed column widths and image-text integration.

websites.name: Field
websites.design_notes: Field's portfolio is dominated by full-bleed generative art video loops in muted pastel palettes (sage greens, dusty pinks). Type is a single grotesque sans used very small (14-18px) so the work dominates. Project tiles are silent video loops that play on hover. No CTAs above the fold — the entire homepage is a gallery.

websites.name: Hellohikimori
websites.design_notes: Hellohikimori uses a black background with a single bright accent color that rotates project-by-project (red, yellow, blue). Type is a custom monospace display face with playful character substitutions. Navigation is hidden behind a single fullscreen menu with large animated text. Each project page uses a different layout — no shared template.

websites.name: Sara Soueidan
websites.design_notes: Sara's portfolio leads with her writing and speaking work, not a list of clients. Dark theme (#111111 bg) with teal accent (#3EB1C8) — the teal was chosen for accessibility on dark backgrounds. Type is Open Sans for body with custom display weights for headlines. Each article card on the homepage shows reading time, topic, and a subtle hover state.

websites.name: Daniel Spatzek
websites.design_notes: Spatzek's portfolio is full of CSS-only experimental art — animated gradients, 3D transforms, and pattern animations rendered entirely in CSS. Color palette is candy-bright (neon greens, hot pinks, electric blues) against black. Each project is presented inside a different experimental frame — some look like TV screens, some like gallery walls. The page itself rotates color themes on each visit.

websites.name: Tobias van Schneider
websites.design_notes: Tobias uses huge black headlines on a clean white background with no images above the fold. Typography mixes a heavy display sans for headlines with a serif body face — the contrast is intentional and signature. Navigation is a single horizontal row of links, all in caps with tight tracking. Every page ends with a personal note written in serif italic.

websites.name: Viget
websites.design_notes: Viget's site reads like a publication — featured articles are above the fold alongside work. Light theme with a single saturated accent (Viget green-blue). Type uses a humanist sans with generous line-height for body content. Work case studies are long-form articles with images, pull quotes, and result metrics — not image galleries.

websites.name: Just Creative
websites.design_notes: Jacob Cass's portfolio is heavy on logo design work shown in tight grid layouts. Pure black-on-white with a single yellow accent (#FFD60A) used for hover states and CTAs. Type is a strong grotesque sans for headlines and body — no serif anywhere. Logo case studies use before/after sliders and process GIFs.

websites.name: Jason Santa Maria
websites.design_notes: Jason's site pioneered per-article custom design — every blog post has its own typography, color palette, and layout. The portfolio homepage is editorial with featured articles in mixed-column layouts. Body type is a readable serif at 18-20px with generous 1.6 line-height. Each project gets its own bespoke presentation, no shared template.

websites.name: Mantinela
websites.design_notes: Mantinela's portfolio uses warm neutral backgrounds with confident typography as the primary visual. Headlines are set in a strong display sans at large sizes — type IS the design. Project thumbnails use a tight grid with consistent aspect ratios and minimal captions. Hover states are subtle (slight color shifts, no scale transforms).

websites.name: Daniel Mall
websites.design_notes: Dan Mall uses a warm cream background with a single saturated mint accent (#00B398) and a clean grotesque sans throughout. Homepage features articles and speaking engagements as primary content — work is secondary. Article pages use full-bleed images with overlaid text in editorial fashion. Speaking section uses a card grid of upcoming talks with date, location, and topic.

websites.name: Pentagram
websites.design_notes: Pentagram's site is editorial-first — work is presented in long-form case studies with large images and minimal chrome. Pure black-on-white with one accent color used per partner section. Typography is a clean grotesque sans with a wide range of weights — no decorative faces. Navigation is unusually partner-led: each of 22 partners has their own section.

websites.name: Collins
websites.design_notes: Collins uses aggressive kinetic typography — text moves, stretches, and rotates on scroll. Black background with full-spectrum color used as accents. Custom display typeface created for the brand. Hero features full-bleed video of their work with overlaid text that animates in. Section transitions are dramatic camera moves, not fades.

websites.name: MediaMonks
websites.design_notes: MediaMonks purple (#8C61FF) on white as the dominant palette with a quirky display sans for headlines. Homepage features a horizontal-scrolling project carousel instead of vertical sections. Office locations are surfaced as a key brand element — a world map showing all 12 offices. Project tiles use a hover-reveal pattern where the project type slides up over the image.

websites.name: UNIT9
websites.design_notes: UNIT9 uses a clean dark theme with a single bright accent (electric blue) and large editorial typography. Hero features a full-bleed looping video of recent work. Project grid is filtered by capability (VR, AR, film, installation) at the top — capabilities are surfaced as the primary nav, not services. Each project page has a credits list at the bottom — every team member named.

websites.name: Stink Studios
websites.design_notes: Stink Studios uses an editorial-spread layout — text and image interlock like a magazine page. Pure black text on white with a single hot-pink accent for hover states. Headlines are an editorial serif (Tiempos) at large sizes, paired with a clean sans for UI. Project list is presented as a long-form table with year, client, and project name.

websites.name: Hello Monday
websites.design_notes: Hello Monday uses warm pastel colors (peach, lavender, mint) on a cream background — completely avoids the dark agency aesthetic. Mascot character (a smiling face) appears throughout the site in different contexts. Type is a custom rounded sans that feels friendly and approachable. Project pages use a monday morning meeting tone — written in first person from the team.

websites.name: Matter
websites.design_notes: Matter's site uses an editorial layout with strong typography and minimal imagery. Pure black on white with a single accent color used per project. Headlines use a custom display sans at large sizes with intentional kerning. Project pages present work as long-form case studies with mixed media — typography, photography, and process documentation.

websites.name: Locomotive
websites.design_notes: Locomotive's site is famously experimental — uses custom GLSL shaders for backgrounds, smooth-scroll library that overrides native scroll, and dramatic text reveals. Dark background with one accent color per project. Type is a single grotesque sans used in a wide range of weights and sizes. Section transitions are 1-2 second animations, not instant.

websites.name: Active Theory
websites.design_notes: Active Theory's site is itself a WebGL experience — full-screen 3D scenes, particle effects, and cinematic camera moves between sections. Color palette is dominated by deep blues and purples with bright accents. Loading screen is a custom WebGL animation that doubles as a brand moment. Project tiles are presented as 3D cards that rotate on hover.

websites.name: Mucho
websites.design_notes: Mucho uses a black-and-white palette with one accent color (yellow #F2C200) used sparingly for emphasis. Typography is a custom display face with character — letters have unusual proportions that feel intentional. Project pages are presented as case studies with a single hero image and a long-form text column. Offices are listed at the top with city and team size — global presence is the first thing you see.

websites.name: Aesop
websites.design_notes: Aesop's ecommerce site reads like a literary publication — product descriptions use long, considered sentences and literary references. Warm neutral palette (cream, sage, soft brown) with no saturated color. Type is a serif (Söhne Breit for headers, paired with a body serif) and is the dominant visual element. Product photography is shot on neutral backgrounds with consistent lighting.

websites.name: Glossier
websites.design_notes: Glossier's signature millennial-pink (#FEEEE7) background with soft pastel accents. Type is a custom sans (Glossier) with rounded terminals and friendly proportions. Product photography uses real people with skin textures visible — no retouching. Hero features UGC (user-generated content) — real customers with the product, not studio shots.

websites.name: Nike
websites.design_notes: Nike uses full-bleed athletic photography as the dominant visual — every page is anchored by a hero image of an athlete in motion. Black and white type with a single accent color (Volt yellow #FEFE33) used sparingly. Typography is bold condensed sans (Nike Sans Condensed) — feels fast and athletic. Product detail pages feature 360-degree spin and video alongside still images.

websites.name: Patagonia
websites.design_notes: Patagonia leads with environmental activism content above product — homepage features conservation campaigns before any product. Earthy color palette (forest green, slate gray, clay) with no neon or saturated colors. Type is a clean humanist sans for body with a slab serif for editorial headers. Product photography always shows the gear in real outdoor environments, not studios.

websites.name: Allbirds
websites.design_notes: Allbirds uses a cream background with a single muted accent color per collection (sage, dusty rose, navy). Type is a friendly rounded sans for body with a display serif for product names. Product photography is shot on neutral seamless backgrounds with the shoe centered — consistent across all products. Sustainability metrics (carbon footprint) are displayed on every product card.

websites.name: Mejuri
websites.design_notes: Mejuri uses warm cream background (#F5F1EA) with deep brown text — no black anywhere. Type is a high-contrast serif (custom) for product names and a clean sans for body. Product photography is shot on warm tan seamless with consistent lighting across all products. Hero features a single product shot, not a lifestyle image — let the product speak.

websites.name: Outdoor Voices
websites.design_notes: OV uses color-blocked section backgrounds (sage, lavender, peach) instead of photography as the primary visual. Type is a heavy condensed sans (Recoleta Semibold) for headlines and a clean sans for body. Product photography uses real people doing non-athletic things in athletic gear (reading, hanging out) — reframing athletic as everyday.

websites.name: SSENSE
websites.design_notes: SSENSE uses pure white background with stark black text and zero accent color. Type is a single grotesque sans (Helvetica Now) used at all sizes — discipline is the brand. Product photography is shot on stark gray seamless with flat lighting — uniform across thousands of products. Editorial content is mixed in with product listings — the line between magazine and shop is intentionally blurred.

websites.name: Mr Porter
websites.design_notes: Mr Porter's site reads like a men's magazine — editorial articles sit alongside product listings. Type is a classic serif (Caslon) for editorial and a clean sans for product UI. Color palette is dominated by navy and white with no bright accents. Product detail pages feature long-form styling advice alongside the product specs.

websites.name: END Clothing
websites.design_notes: END uses pure black-on-white with a single accent color that rotates seasonally. Type is a bold grotesque sans (Founders Grotesk) at large sizes — feels confident and streetwear-appropriate. Product photography is shot on white seamless with flat lighting — consistent across all brands. Homepage features a single full-bleed image of a current campaign, not a product grid.

websites.name: The New York Times
websites.design_notes: NYT uses a custom Cheltenham serif for headlines and a body serif (Imperial) — type IS the brand. Headlines can be 48-72px and dominate above the fold. Layout uses a 6-column grid on desktop with intentional asymmetry — breaking the grid for featured stories. Photography is treated editorially with full-bleed images and overlaid captions.

websites.name: The Verge
websites.design_notes: The Verge uses a distinctive purple accent paired with red in their visual identity. Type mixes a strong display sans for headlines with a clean body serif. Article layouts use unusual element arrangements — embedded media breaks the column grid. Heavy use of pull quotes and inline media throughout articles.

websites.name: Bloomberg
websites.design_notes: Bloomberg uses a dense layout — multiple stories and data widgets visible above the fold. Type is a custom sans (Bloomberg Sans) designed for screen density. Live market data appears in the header — financial information is treated as part of the brand chrome. Article pages use a narrow column (600px) for body text but allow wider elements to break out for charts and tables.

websites.name: Awwwards
websites.design_notes: Awwwards uses a dark background with a single bright accent (yellow #FFFF00 used sparingly) for hover and CTAs. Type is a clean grotesque sans with very tight tracking on headlines. Site of the Day is the primary content — large card with full screenshot. Submissions are shown in a 3-column grid with hover-to-expand detail panels.

websites.name: Siteinspire
websites.design_notes: Siteinspire is aggressively minimal — pure white background, black text, no accent color. Site cards are presented in a 4-column grid with screenshot + minimal metadata (style, type, subject, platform). Filter sidebar uses checkboxes and tags — no dropdowns. Type is a single grotesque sans at small sizes throughout — the work is the only visual.

websites.name: Land-book
websites.design_notes: Land-book specializes in landing pages — every entry is a single landing page screenshot. Dark background with white cards, each showing one screenshot with title and tags. Categories (Type, Industry, Style) are surfaced as a primary nav — filtering is the core UX. Type is a single sans (Inter) at small sizes, with screenshot captions in muted gray.

websites.name: Httpster
websites.design_notes: Httpster uses an unusual horizontal-scrolling layout — newest sites are presented in a horizontal river at the top. Pure white background with bold black type. Each site card is just a screenshot + site name — no metadata. Filtering is minimal: only by category, no tag system. Type is a strong display sans for site names, sans for everything else.

websites.name: Codrops
websites.design_notes: Codrops uses a clean editorial layout with code blocks treated as primary visual elements. Dark background for code blocks, light background for body — sharp contrast. Type is a humanist sans for body with monospace for code. Each article features a live demo at the top — interactive examples before any explanation. Heavy use of diagrams and inline illustrations to explain concepts.

websites.name: Typewolf
websites.design_notes: Typewolf is curated typography — every site featured has typography as its primary design element. Type is a custom sans (Martin) used at small sizes — the typography being showcased is the visual. Each entry shows a screenshot with the typeface names called out below in muted gray. Trend reports use long-form text with embedded examples, not image grids.

websites.name: Sidebar
websites.design_notes: Sidebar is a daily design link list — 5 links per day, every day. Pure white background with black text and a single blue accent for links. Layout is a single column of dated entries — no grid, no cards. Type is a humanist sans (Source Sans) for body with bold for link titles. Each link has a one-sentence description in muted gray — extreme editorial discipline.

websites.name: Plausible Analytics
websites.design_notes: Plausible's dashboard shows everything on one screen — no tabs, no secondary nav. Top bar has traffic sources as colored bars, then visitor count, then a top-pages list. Color palette is muted (sage green, slate blue, warm gray) with no neon. Type is a humanist sans (Inter) at small sizes with high contrast for legibility. The dashboard is so simple it fits in a screenshot — that's the design statement.

websites.name: Retool
websites.design_notes: Retool's marketing site shows a real Retool-built dashboard as the hero — split-screen with builder UI on left, live app on right. Color palette is muted (blue primary #3D5AFE, grays) with one accent. Type is a clean sans (Inter) at multiple weights. The product UI itself uses a strict component system — every panel, button, and table uses the same token system.

websites.name: Mixpanel
websites.design_notes: Mixpanel's marketing shows real dashboards with charts (funnels, retention curves, segmentation). Color palette uses a saturated purple (#7856FF) as primary with semantic colors (green for positive, red for negative) in data viz. Type is a humanist sans (Graphik) at multiple weights. Data viz uses a consistent palette across all chart types — chart literacy is a design priority.

websites.name: Amplitude
websites.design_notes: Amplitude uses a confident blue (#1F61E5) as primary with a dark navy hero section — enterprise feel. Headlines use a strong grotesque sans (Söhne) at large sizes. The product UI screenshots show real analytics dashboards with multiple chart types stacked — emphasis on the depth of analysis possible. Customer logos are presented as a trusted-by wall, very enterprise-coded.

websites.name: Metabase
websites.design_notes: Metabase uses a friendly palette (purple and blue on white) with rounded UI elements — feels approachable for non-technical users. Mascot illustrations appear throughout (a small owl character) in different poses. Type is a humanist sans (Inter) with rounded terminals. Dashboard screenshots show a question-builder UI — emphasizing that you don't need SQL to use it.

websites.name: Stripe Docs
websites.design_notes: Stripe Docs are the gold standard — every code example is interactive (you can switch languages, copy, and run real API calls). Three-column layout: left nav for sections, middle for prose, right for code examples that stay sticky. Type is Söhne for body with a monospace (Stripe Sans Mono) for code. Code blocks have language tabs at the top and a copy button at the top right.

websites.name: React Docs
websites.design_notes: React's new docs (post-2023) lead with interactive sandboxes — every code example can be edited and run inline. Dark theme default with a soft blue (#087EA4) accent. Type is a humanist sans for body with a monospace (Cascadia Code) for code. Layout uses a left sidebar with a Learn/Reference split — the learning path is intentional, not just API reference.

websites.name: Tailwind CSS Docs
websites.design_notes: Tailwind docs use a sidebar nav grouped by category (Layout, Flexbox & Grid, Spacing, etc.) — the structure mirrors the mental model of CSS. Code blocks show both the class and the rendered output side-by-side. Type is Inter for body with a monospace (JetBrains Mono) for code. Color swatches appear inline in the docs — you can see the color of bg-blue-500 next to its name.

websites.name: Cloudflare Docs
websites.design_notes: Cloudflare docs use a left nav with product grouping (Workers, Pages, R2, D1, etc.) — each product has its own docs section with a different visual treatment. Color is Cloudflare orange (#F38020) accent on white. Type is Inter for body with a monospace for code. Search is prominently placed at the top — for a 1000+ page docs site, search is the primary nav.

websites.name: Notion API Docs
websites.design_notes: Notion's API docs are written in Notion-style — emoji icons on every section header, callout blocks, and toggle lists. Light theme with Notion's signature black text on near-white. Type is a clean sans (Inter) for body with a monospace for code. Code examples are presented in copy-able blocks with language tabs. The whole thing feels like a Notion doc — meta and friendly.

websites.name: Apple
websites.design_notes: Apple's product pages use full-bleed product photography as the dominant visual — text is sparse and confident. Color palette per product (iPhone page uses deep blue, MacBook page uses silver gradients). Type is SF Pro Display for headlines (huge, 80-120px) and SF Pro Text for body. Scroll-triggered animations show product features in motion — the page is a vertical film reel of product moments.

websites.name: Stripe Press
websites.design_notes: Stripe Press uses a warm cream background (#FAF7F2) with deep charcoal text and a single saturated accent per book. Type is a custom serif (Söhne Breit) for headlines and a body serif for descriptions — the entire site feels like a hardback book. Each book has its own landing page with a different layout — no template. Book photography is shot on warm neutral backgrounds with consistent lighting.

websites.name: Basecamp
websites.design_notes: Basecamp's homepage is famously long-form — single column of text-heavy sections that read like an essay. Yellow accent (#FFD455) on white background with black text. Type is a humanist serif (Tiempos Headline) for headlines and a clean sans for body. Every section makes a strong opinionated claim (project management tools are too complex) — copy is the conversion mechanism.

websites.name: Mailchimp
websites.design_notes: Mailchimp uses bold yellow (#FFE01B) as primary brand color with black text — instantly recognizable. Custom illustrations in a hand-drawn style appear throughout the site. Type is a custom display sans (Mailchimp Sans) with friendly proportions. Hero features a single bold headline and a CTA — minimal above the fold, then dense feature sections below.

websites.name: Headspace
websites.design_notes: Headspace uses warm orange (#F47D31) as primary with soft cream backgrounds — calm and approachable. Custom illustrations of smiling characters (the Headspace mascot) appear throughout. Type is a rounded sans (Sailec) with friendly proportions. Animation is slow and gentle — breathing-pace transitions, not bouncy micro-interactions.

websites.name: Duolingo
websites.design_notes: Duolingo uses their mascot (Duo the owl) throughout the site in different poses — the mascot IS the brand. Color palette is bright greens (#58CC02) with secondary colors per character. Type is a custom rounded sans with extremely bold weights. Illustrations are flat-color with thick outlines — picture book aesthetic.

websites.name: Mubi
websites.design_notes: Mubi uses a dark background (#0E0E0E) with full-bleed film stills as the primary visual. Type is a custom display serif (Mubi Grotesk) at large sizes — feels cinematic. Featured film of the day is huge above the fold. Film pages use editorial-style layouts with critic quotes, director bios, and curated context — not just metadata.

websites.name: A24
websites.design_notes: A24 uses pure black background with white text — monochrome discipline. Type is a custom display sans at huge sizes (often 100px+). Film pages are minimal: poster image, title, release date, and a single tagline — no synopsis, no trailer auto-play. Limited edition merch is surfaced prominently alongside film content — the studio is also a brand.

websites.name: Metamask
websites.design_notes: Metamask uses orange (#F6851B) as primary with a dark blue/charcoal background. Fox mascot (the Metamask fox) appears in 3D renders throughout the site. Type is a clean sans (IBM Plex Sans) for body with a monospace for technical details. Hero features a 3D animated fox that follows the cursor — playful but functional.

websites.name: Polygon
websites.design_notes: Polygon uses purple (#8247E5) as primary on white background with bold geometric patterns as section dividers. Type is a clean sans (Poly Sans — custom) at large sizes. Hero features an animated geometric pattern (the Polygon logo shape) that morphs on scroll. Web3 jargon is minimized — the marketing is accessible to non-crypto audiences.

websites.name: Mozilla
websites.design_notes: Mozilla uses a bright color palette (orange, teal, pink) on white background — playful but credible. Type is a custom sans (Zilla Slab for headlines, Inter for body) — Zilla Slab is the brand element. Hero features a manifesto-style headline about internet health, not a product. Multiple campaigns are surfaced on the homepage — Mozilla is a multi-initiative organization.

websites.name: Wikipedia
websites.design_notes: Wikipedia is aggressively utilitarian — pure white background with black text and blue links. Type is a serif (Linux Libertine for headlines, sans for body) — encyclopedic and serious. The homepage is famously just a logo, a search bar, and links to language editions. Article pages use a single column of text with a sidebar table of contents — focus on reading.

websites.name: Internet Archive
websites.design_notes: Internet Archive uses a dense information-rich layout — multiple sections visible above the fold (Wayback Machine, books, movies, audio). Type is a humanist sans for body with a slab serif (Playfair) for section headers. Color palette uses a deep maroon (#222222 with maroon accents) — library aesthetic. Stats are prominently displayed (625 billion web pages archived) — proof of scale.

websites.name: EFF
websites.design_notes: EFF uses a deep blue (#005C9C) as primary on white background — credible and serious. Type is a humanist sans (Source Sans Pro) for body with bold weights for headlines. Hero features a current campaign with a clear call-to-action (sign petition, donate). Blog posts are surfaced alongside activism campaigns — the line between news and action is intentionally blurred.

websites.name: Charity: Water
websites.design_notes: Charity: Water uses a clean light background with warm yellow (#FFD455) as primary accent. Type is a humanist sans (Proxima Nova) at large sizes for headlines. Photography is documentary-style — real people in real places, not stock. The 100 percent model (100 percent of donations go to water projects) is repeated throughout the site — proof of integrity is the brand message.

websites.name: Maggie Appleton
websites.design_notes: Maggie's site is a digital garden — notes are organized by growth status (seedling, budding, evergreen) with visual icons. Warm cream background (#FCFAF4) with hand-drawn illustrations throughout. Type is a humanist serif (Söhne) for body with handwritten accents. The garden structure is the navigation — you explore notes by their stage of growth, not by date.

websites.name: Andy Matuschak
websites.design_notes: Andy's site is structured around evergreen notes — each note is a self-contained concept, not a blog post. Pure white background with black text and a single muted accent color. Type is a clean sans (IBM Plex Sans) for body with a serif (Concourse) for headlines. Notes are heavily cross-linked — the site is a network of concepts, not a chronological feed.

websites.name: Gwern
websites.design_notes: Gwern's site is famously dense — long-form essays with extensive footnotes, citations, and inline data visualizations. Black text on white with minimal color (links are dark blue, footnotes are gray). Type is a serif (ETBembo) for body with sans for UI. Essays include interactive elements (dropdowns for citations, hover-able footnotes) — designed for deep reading.

websites.name: Paul Graham
websites.design_notes: Paul Graham's site is intentionally old-web — plain HTML, default browser styles, and a simple top-aligned table of essays. Type is Verdana (the system sans) at default sizes — no custom fonts. Background is white, text is black, links are blue. Essays are the only content — no bio photo, no social links, no portfolio. The minimalism IS the brand statement.

websites.name: Derek Sivers
websites.design_notes: Derek's site uses a pure white background with black text and a single accent color (currently red — Derek changes the accent occasionally). Type is a serif (Bookmania) for body with a sans for UI. Layout is single-column throughout — every page is just a title and prose. No images, no sidebars, no CTAs — focus is entirely on the writing.

websites.name: Craig Mod
websites.design_notes: Craig's site uses a clean serif (Tiempos Text) for body with a sans for UI. Layout uses an unusually narrow column (540px max) for body text — extreme focus on reading. Photography is presented in full-bleed black-and-white with no captions inline (captions at the bottom). Newsletter sign-up is the only CTA — books and essays are the primary content.

websites.name: Tom MacWright
websites.design_notes: Tom's site uses a single sans (system-ui) at default sizes — no custom fonts. Pure black text on white with no accent color. Layout is a single column with no sidebar — focus on content. Posts are tagged and dated but no tag cloud or archive widget. The whole site reads like a Markdown file rendered with minimal styling.

websites.name: Robin Rendle
websites.design_notes: Robin's site uses a warm cream background (#FBF7F0) with deep charcoal text. Type is a custom serif (Robin's own) for body with a sans for UI. Layout uses a wide main column with intentional asymmetry — text and image interlock. Newsletter signup is integrated into the footer, not a popup — gentle, not aggressive.

websites.name: Frank Chimero
websites.design_notes: Frank's site uses a warm off-white background (#F5F0E6) with deep brown text — no black anywhere. Type is a serif (Tiempos Headline) for headlines and a sans for body. The homepage features a current thing (often a book or project) front and center, with links to writing and work as secondary nav. Layout uses generous whitespace and an unusual asymmetric grid.

websites.name: Patrick Collison
websites.design_notes: Patrick's site uses a pure white background with black text and minimal color (blue links). Type is a serif (Iowan Old Style) for body — feels book-like. Layout is single column with no sidebar — focus entirely on reading. Content is heavily indexed by topic — long lists of links to essays and book reviews are the primary navigation.
